I was so embarrassed and I was like I will never speak again that was the worst most painful experience in front of so many people and and then and then About that time is when tele seminars came out and so I started to tell seminars and what was cool As I started learning tell seminars some of the people I was in tell seminars But they had they had a structure like the actual framework I remember I got his call I was doing a tell seminar with the guy named Mike Chen and before he got me on the phone He said okay this tell seminars gonna be 60 minutes long He's like these are the five questions that need you to ask me because these are the concerns people have in each These questions were resolved a different concern and the end of it I need ten minutes to do my pitch and I got away.
I closed people at the end and he had this framework I was like okay, and so I didn't know what to do but I just interviewed him and I followed his framework and Like I just remember we we made I can't remember how much money made it seemed like a lot back in probably a couple thousand bucks I was just like holy crap like it worked And so then I was like that's my frameworks and I started doing tell seminars the same way he would do and I would I would Ask questions that would resolve concerns and I would kind of structure that way and also it started working And then I got invited to another seminar so that's how I did the seminar and this time I structured my presentation very similar to that and I had like a ten minute close at the end and actually made some sales and Then I started getting addicted to and I started going To seminars again, and I spent about two or three years on the road At least once a week traveling somewhere in the country to do a seminar and I would go and I would sit there I would watch all the other presenters on stage I would take notes not what they were teaching but how they were doing it what they did their transition how they do their Closes and I start taking notes on that I started weaving these things in my presentation And after three years after three years, I realized I hated being on the road I had twin boys at home who were little babies.

I was scared to sell high ticket It was a weakness of mine But you know, I I was launching all these products and I got tired of doing it and then getting paid after I created the Product so I get this idea one day.
I said listen, I want to create this product But I want to do it live similar to what we're doing right now So what I did was I emailed my listeners to listen if you come to this webinar you get the product for free I won't even charge you anything if you don't come to this webinar and you want this product You'll have to pay for it later But I'm going to create the product live on the fly and back then I was using mind maps I think I should bring that back by the way because I would literally do the whole through It was a three and a half hour session on and it was a mind map And I'd break out the bubbles and it was all about time management for our internet marketers Which is not a great topic, but for 37 bucks a lot of people at the time.
They're like, okay I'll check that out or better if they come online.
They get it for free.
So I do this webinar It goes incredibly well and I get testimonials right there on the webinar And so I screenshot of those testimonials I go back to my audience the next day and say hey, you could have came for free You didn't I'm going to sell this product for 37 dollars.
However, I'm going to give you a second chance because you're an email subscriber of mine I'll let you for the next 48 hours get it for 27 dollars and I sold that product in insane amounts I was like, oh my god, you know, this is incredible I literally sold a product that they could have got for free if they would have just acted earlier They didn't but this, you know, it's like reverse reciprocity.
I try to do you a favor you neglected me I'm asking you a second time for a favor.
Yeah, literally.
This is a psychological trigger Um, you know reverse reciprocity or negative reciprocity, but here's what's really great about this Russell And here's what I discovered.
I pre frame my audience to equate Me doing webinars with tremendous value.
So Before I ever sold them anything they had the experience of doing a webinar with me And getting really great value if they got it for free great if they got it for a deal They knew that I delivered substance on my webinars now later on I was like, oh my god, that's brilliant But at the time it was just an accident and so my audience is first experience with doing webinars And this is a mistake I see a lot of people making is the first webinar they ever do their audience is a pitch webinar And it's not done very well and their audience now associates them with like a used car salesman And they say I'm going to stay away from your webinars Because I didn't get value out of it because it was clunky or it was hard or whatever, right?
Uh, so then you would think I would be smart enough to immediately jump in and start doing sales pitch webinars But I wasn't here's what I did next and again by accident It's you know, if more people thought this way about click funnels Russell We'd all be a lot richer because you know go to webinars a 30 day trial and I'm like, okay.
It's day 29 I use this webinar technology once I made some money with it But I have no plans to use it again.

Yeah They were most of them were cheesy and I never wanted to sell like that I never felt comfortable selling like that And I always felt that there's that that was good for the short term But it wasn't necessarily a long-term advantage that I could maintain because essentially in most pitches even today on webinars and in seminars wherever they may be they're all Here's what you need to do And then buy my thing and I'll show you how to do it And and that's like getting somebody all dressed up with no place to go I always thought if we could show them what it's like to be our customer before they are our customer And then we give them an opportunity where it costs them more not to be our customer in time and effort And money that they would spend otherwise that they would lose Then naturally unless they're stupid, they're going to become our customers And and so my webinar formula that I designed based on that was If I'm I started selling them webinar stuff training on a webinar But I sold them the webinar first and then from that we figured out how to sell everything else with the webinar So people were asking can I sell coaching?

He always sounds optimistic That's you know tonality can go a long ways to the sell We'll definitely talk about that and and because I'm interested in and knowing how you get in state when you do webinars Because I think it's an important thing that a lot of people don't talk about but so, you know The conclusion on my thing is when I finally figured out I could sell webinars and everything else My whole my whole focus and this is the thing that killed me at first Is I overtaught and I didn't create I created all this value and the people say that was great You know, this is my favorite thing That was the best moment I've ever been Jason and then I looked their name up in my shopping cart It wasn't there and I'd say well clearly it was it because you'd say that was great Jason and then they go and spend money with somebody else Somebody didn't like right the only only opinion I care is that people vote with their credit card like that's right With their credit card.
I don't trust their opinion Yeah, and you know, it's like you can tell real behavior based on based on buying patterns not based on anything else beside everything else Secondary to that, you know, so so this is my breakthrough And then I'd love to hear yours on this whole concept too because it is a balance If you over educate you're going to create more confusion in the audience You're going to give them more reasons to say well Let me put in to work what you just taught me see how it goes and then I'll come back and that's the greatest lie of all time Right, uh, and you know the other thing too is if you don't provide value you can sell without providing value I see guys do it every single day the problem with that is it's ultimately going to lead to a letdown with the customer experience And so I I'm like it's harder to find a new audience than it is to create a strong pitch I would rather I can do a lot with a little you give me a hundred people that have some nice powerful disposable income And I'll make a million dollars But if you give me a million people and and we don't treat them right I can't even make a hundred dollars and sleep well at night with them And so the the real balance and and we talked about this you and I talk about this a lot this is my big breakthrough is If I can create a new identity and I'm sure people who followed you have listened to this, right?
If I could create an identity that empowered the person To be able to solve the problem that previously they couldn't solve I have now empowered them to look at everything from a new perspective And therefore if I could align my offer within that identity To accent accelerate that identity Then I'm going to make a sell because people typically are going to act in a way that is consistent with their identity This is called commitment inconsistency So somebody believes that they're brave if they're a risk taker if there's somebody that's open to new things If there's somebody that's willing to put the time and effort in to succeed And then I sell them something that checks all those boxes done But if I sell them that thing and I've created this or I haven't changed the identity they have of themselves or reinforced A an impoverished identity Then the problem is is I could sell them hundred dollar bills for ten dollars and they won't want it because they don't they can't see themselves If they see themselves as somebody who fails at everything that they do And you're selling them hope and optimism You've lost It's done.
It's over with right and so so every piece of content I do and by the way as an educator anybody here who wants to be an expert or who is an expert That's what you do when you teach if you're good Now you teach through identity shifts because you can when somebody spends money with you Don't listen to you talk for a hundred hours Because they've already made a commitment.
They said this is important.
Why is this important because I spent money with it So therefore I will make time for it But these same people will say nobody comes to a two and a half hour webinar Nobody has time for that but then they listen to 25 hours of podcasts a week, right?
It's like they will pay attention to what they pay for and so you get identity shifts either consciously or accidentally Over a repeated exposure to new concepts over and over again in their mind in your education Our goal is how do we take a 25 hour education induction experience and put it into 45 minutes So they can see that experience shift as they can feel it if they can see that new identity force that new identity And then that leads to the sell.
So my my ultimate way I do this is very covert Uh, and this is really interesting.
We need to definitely dive deep further between overt selling and covert selling Uh, you know, you know the difference I I talk about there is overt selling essentially says, you know Hey, do you want to die poor or do you want to have something to leave on to your children?
What I'm giving you today is something that will allow you to create a legacy that will outlive you That's a close right now.
By the way, everybody should have hundreds of clothes is memorized Now that's direct overt selling.
What am I doing?
I'm pushing the pain button on being broke And I'm pushing the dopamine pleasure button On being rich and then I'm giving an offer that can help you get rich and that's good You have to sell like that But a covert selling situation might be you know, listen There are two types of people that I encounter in this world One is the type was when they're recognized with a good idea and intuition hits them They seem to follow it and move forward even if they don't have all the information The second type of person is you could give them every piece of information in the world You can get a 99.999999999% of everything they need to know and they'll still not move forward Because of that one tiny fraction of something that doesn't even matter that holds them up from realizing a good opportunity Now I don't know who you are, but I can tell you who I can help And I can help these people the most from the blood, sweat, and tears that I put into this product over here.
I didn't say you're one of these people who can act that way, right?
I have covertly set up a situation to allow you to step into that identity and then you can move forward with it.
Now covert selling is super duper advanced and it's something you have to get good at over time because if you don't ask directly, you run the risk of them not understanding what to do and and you never want to have ambiguity.
You tell people, I want you to go and buy this right now.
But you also want to, if you want as much as possible, to convert it.
So in my framework, Russell, when I teach four steps, I'm doing a very seductive dance here.
I say, here's the four step process on how to do blank.
And people think I'm giving them education and I am.
They think that's all I'm giving them though.
That's the superficiality of it.
They say, well, I come to a webinar to learn.
I come to a webinar to find out information and education.
I know you're going to sell me at the end, but not me.
I'm not going to buy.
I'm just going to learn for free on your efforts.
Right.
And instead of resisting that or fighting against that, I lean into that.
But what they don't realize is every piece of content that I use and teach and instruct is purely built around identity shifts.
It's purely built around giving them insight into seeing things from a new perspective that empowers them as opposed to the current perspective, which limits them and they never know I'm doing this.
And by the way, once they find out I'm doing this, it doesn't even matter because it's still effective, but I never outright say, I'm going to empower you and give you a new identity because then it doesn't seem to work.
But if I can sneak that in.
I wrote a book talking about this and people still worked.

I mean, you can deconstruct.

Right.
We couldn't, we couldn't stop ourselves because what was our identity?
Because essentially our identity was tight.
It was tied up as somebody who wasn't able to do the things they needed to make their health a priority.
Our identity was tied to resisting change when it came to the habits that we had formed around our eating.
And we could go on and on about all these different things that occur.
And you might ask, well, where do we find these?
Well, we'll talk about where we find these here in a little bit.
But the idea is my identity shifted when I started hanging out with a lot more people that made it a priority that were entrepreneurs who were also very big into fitness.
I started noticing there was this high, this is so, everything's a business thing for me.
Right.
I'm like, oh my God, there's a market over here of all these entrepreneurs that are teaching at the health field and they're just killing it.
I want to partner with these guys.
But if I come in there like with my candy bars and with my gluten enhanced food, right?
I could give more business if I looked like them.
It's all my identity started shifting to if I'm going to make, if I want to impact and help over here.
And by the way, my identity also shifted because I could see over and over again.
These were people who clearly figured out a balance between everything.
And therefore I made it a priority.
And now I saw it as I'm not trying to lose weight.
That was my identity before I shifted my identity to somebody who was going to make a life out of being optimal in my nutrition, optimal in my experience, my day to day health.
And that identity shift happened and automatically the weight loss occurred as a result of that.
Now, can you do that in 45 minutes on a webinar?
Not exactly, right?
But if you can get somebody the first leverage point, if you can get them to say, because here's the biggest dilemma that we have.
Most people in the audience listed today, most you and I, when we talk about this, Russell, the audiences that we reach who we attempt to sell, they probably, most of them have failed a thousand times or 10,000 times at the very thing that we're promising them.
So, hey, you want to make money online?
Okay, well, I've done the opposite of that.
You know, I've spent tens of thousand dollars on courses, including yours, right?
And Jason, you're telling me straight face that this time it's going to be different, even though the last 999 times that I tried something I failed.
It's amazing that a sell ever occurs in the face of that cold-hearted logic.
Logically, it doesn't make any sense for you to attempt something that you tried 999 times and failed.
The biggest identity shift you have in an audience like that is to getting to realize that this time can in fact be different, that the past doesn't matter in the sense of its limitations.
Once you can get them to see that the future doesn't have to be the past, once you can see that, it doesn't matter how many times you failed, you only need to succeed once.

It was like, what is their false belief that's holding him back?
I need to break that false belief and rebuild it with the root, with the true belief that they have that, then they have to give me money.
Um, I'll give a super fast example that'll illustrate this.
Um, because I think it's, it's easy one to explain, but let's say, um, you're a network marketer, right?
And so if you're trying to pitch somebody on an, on an org, on a company, right?
If I came to you and I'm like, Hey, you should join, uh, my new company where I sell blah, whatever it is, right?
You have, most people have internal false beliefs already around that, right?
Like, um, and usually the false beliefs initially is like, uh, if I, if I join you in that remarketing thing, I'm going to lose my friends, my family.
Everyone's going to hate me.
It's going to be horrible.
And so I got to think back like, why did they actually believe that?
Like what is the reason that they believe that thing?
And it's like, well, something happened in their life.
They probably either joined a network marketing program, called their friends and family, got rejected.
Now they had so much pain that they associate.
Like if I do this, I'm going to be in pain or they're on the other end of it, where their brother or their mom or their sister signed up and they got called every single day for like six months until they changed their number by someone they love.
And like that was a horrible experience.
I don't want that, right?
So if I'm going to come to someone and try to present network marketing, they did this falsely.
I don't care how good my product is, my service, my like, they, they don't care because they have this false belief that if I join your network marketing company, it's going to cause me so much pain.
Like I don't want to do it.
So I don't, I don't have to sell anybody on end product right now.
All I gotta do is I got to convince them that this is, that this belief that they're holding true is not actually true.
So how do I do that?
Well, if I was in network marketing, I would think back in my history.
Like what was, why don't I believe that?
Like something obviously happened.
There's some experience or something that happened.
What was that?
And it's a lot of times it's hard to go back and like really, really think back and, uh, and remember that was.
And then let's say for example, um, uh, like, I'll share my example because I've done an all network marketing.
I'll tell my story.
And this is, this is my origin story.
Right.
So I meet some of the, Hey guys, thank you for coming to this webinar.
Uh, we're going to talk about network marketing.
The first thing I want to, I want to call this out because I know a lot of you guys are like, uh, the money sounds awesome.
Product sounds great, but I don't want to destroy my life.
How many of you guys are worried about like, if I joined a company, I'm going to destroy my life.
Yeah.
Like I felt the exact same way.
In fact, uh, what's interesting is when I drew my very first program, first person, how to get my, how to get my phone out.
I call my friends, my family.
I call my mom.
She, um, she was like really upset that he had approached it.
I called my brother.
He hung up on me, called my sister and she'd already got a text from my brother.
So she didn't even respond to the phone.
And it was like horrible within like an hour and a half.
All my friends and family were alienated and I hated network marketing.
I quit and I swear I'd never do it again.
And everyone's like, yes, that's how I, that's how I feel too.
Right.
I'm like, but then I met this dude and I met this, um, event and he drives up in this car that I'm not a big car guy, but it must have been at least 150,000 or car.
I was like, Hey man, nice car.
And he gets out and I'm like, what do you, what do you do?
It was kind of like that movie, um, uh, remembering happiness where Will Smith comes up and pursue the happiness.
Yeah.
It was like that.
And I was like, dude, what do you do?

I'm like, uh, like I tried that and it was just really, really painful.
And it was horrible.
He's like, he's like, did you call your friends and family?
I'm like, yeah, he's like, no wonder you hate network marketing.
I would have hated it too.

I'm like, yeah.
He's like, I literally put up ads online to say who wants to make money.

I didn't call them on the phone.
I tell them the thing and then they give me money.
Okay.
100% success rate because they tell me to call them to explain how they can make money and then they just give me money.
I was like, are you kidding me?
I don't have to talk about it.
No.
And guess what just happened to me.
I have this, I had this, this belief where I was like, Oh my gosh, I wanted that dream, but now I can do it without actually having to talk to somebody.
Boom.
I'm in.
I don't care what the program is at that point.
The false belief has been destroyed and I'm completely in.
Okay.
And, um, and so that's like, that's the goal of these things is like, what was like for you to believe whatever it is you're selling, what was the story about why you believe, right?

There will be people no matter what that can't afford your thing, even if it was a dollar, but 99% of people who think they can't afford it are essentially saying it's not worth it for me to deploy the resources and revenue to this opportunity, even if they shift the, the identity.
So we have to shift the identity and then we have to also create the identity in terms of that this isn't something that costs money.
This is something that produces money or saves time and every single offer in the world could be made with that argument.

So it's so funny because I knew this was an issue when Russell first tried to sell click funnels as he was talking about funnels.

I mean, I love it when we create competition because it shows us we've created enough shift in the marketplace where people are now basing their business on a position against our business.

I'm just, yep.
Yep.
And this is great.
Cause I think, and I've always, I've always discovered this, the more ways I can look at a solution or sorry, the more ways I can look at something, the different, the more perspectives I can provide on it, the more insight I get.
And a lot of times people and typically all of the audiences that we serve and sell to, they typically look at the, the problem through one lens and that's why they're stuck.
So a big insight on shifting belief patterns is to get them to look at it from a different lens.
You know, somebody says to me, you know, no woman would ever want to go out with me Jason cause I'm bald.
It's laughable.
First of all, that, that decision there that they've made and decided in general lies cause you're like, okay, so, so let me get this straight.
There's not a single person on this earth who is bald that has a woman.
They're all lonely.
They're all single.
Is that correct?
Well, no, that's not correct.
Now we've opened the door for them to look at it from another perspective.
So is it bald?
That's the issue.
Is there something deeper going on here?
And now they start thinking about it from a new perspective, right?
So our idea is to give new perspectives and this is why I like learning from Russell and I think vice versa too is that gives us new insight to do what we're already doing new perspective to have a multi-pronged approach that solving something.
So in this case with belief patterns is with me is like authority is the most important thing I try to do in the first few minutes of a webinar because nobody's going to believe me if they don't perceive me to be the ultimate provider of that solution.
Now here's what's really important.
Cause cause here's it.
Here's it.
Here's an internal belief.
Some people that are listening to this right now are having Russell that I know to be a fact.
They say, well, Jason, I'm just getting started in webinars.
I'm not an authority.
I'm not a super successful person.
I don't have these types of things.
I'm screwed, right?
Or I guess I got to wait until I get them in order to do this.
No, change the rules of the game.
So there is something that anybody in the world could do better than everybody else.
You just have to define what it is.
Right.
So I'm, I'm really big.
I'm never going to climb Mount Everest.
Let's be clear on that, but I'm really interested in reading books on people who have climbed Mount Everest.
And it's fascinating to me because at first it became who's the first person ever to climb Mount Everest, right?
And then he did it, right?
And then it became who was the first American to do it?
Then it was who was the first woman to do it?
Then it was, you know, who was the oldest person to do it?
Who was the youngest person to do it?
The first person with one leg, et cetera, et cetera.
Right.
They create, they kept creating new categories where they could say I was the first or number one.
Well, when you're selling, you can create in your webinar, a, what we call the defined outcome over here, which is the whole purpose behind it is you're going to show them the one thing that you can better help them with than anything else that they could find.
Period.
It's up to you to decide and choose what that is.
And because you set the rules, you can come up with it.
Like the first product I ever sold that I was successful with was a product called how to write an article in seven minutes or less.
It was way back in the easy and article days.
This is like 2007, right?
And by the way, that, that, that I'd never made any money writing articles myself.
I, people had hired me as a ghost writer.
That product didn't even teach people where to submit those articles, how to get traffic from those articles.
It didn't teach them anything other than I had this process for putting out decent articles and a very quick timeframe on a variety of different subjects.
And I took that as my defined outcomes.
I'm going to show you this one very specific, what very narrow thing.
And through that, I can show you just by education alone, if nothing else, that I have the ultimate authority on it.
Cause I've decided and focused on it.
And it's something that nobody else has ever focused on.
Right.
Uh, so, so that's the idea is you can get authority that way.
There's other ways that you could get authority.
One of the easiest ways to get authorities is just take pictures with famous people.
Um, it's like it's authority.
It's celebrity by proxy, right?
Um, you know, speaking itself on a webinar.
Is, is an authoritative thing because most people don't get up and speak in front of audiences, right?
Uh, but, but to the point here is we can't be the broke uncle, given financial advice, we can't be the, the uncle who's been divorced seven times, giving you relationship advice.
And until proven otherwise, your audience is going to think that.
So it's important.
This is why it's important to give authority because it'll help you have leverage to shift their identity.
Cause they'll believe that you're somebody who knows better than them about the topic, right?
Now, one of the things that I like to do, and I do this very deliberately and very specifically, cause I think it's so important.
I don't want to forget it is I create commitment to that new identity that I want to forge throughout that process.
Uh, and so I, this is what I call the mega commitment is I essentially tell somebody, I say, I need you to agree in writing with me right now, before we go any further, that if we do blank, then you will do blank.
Uh, it's a very, very put a box around the thing.
If you will, right?
Uh, so like if I'm selling on, uh, you know, if I was selling click funnels on a webinar, I would essentially say, I'm going to show you the most effective tool vehicle, whatever you want to call it, that is going to make you successful in business and in life.
I am going to show you the very thing that makes all the difference no matter who you are, no matter where you're at, as long as you follow this process.
That's what I'm here to do for you today.
Right.
Now I could just show it to you and hope that you use it, right?
But I don't think that's good enough.
If I were to show this to you and you could see it for yourself and on your own, draw the conclusion that, yes, this is the single greatest, most effective thing I need right now in my business.
Then my question to you is, will you tell me God and everybody else in the world that you will do this, that you won't just think about it or say one day, someday, maybe maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe that you will actually make a commitment to me and more importantly to yourself that you will use this.
If you see the value in it, put yes into the chat box right now, if that's you.
What have I done there?
I've literally created an identity shift in the first few minutes.
The identity shift, though, is done softly.
If I'm able to achieve blank on this webinar today, then you must agree to me to do blank.
There's a lot of psychology behind this.
Thank you.
Yeah, no, no, no, and this is the difference.
People think we kind of just get off the cuff and we do, because we've done this a million times, right?
But if I'm pitching that on a webinar, I might spend five freaking hours developing that frame, right?
I might say, well, how do we put it here?
How do we, every little tiny thing is accounted for and developed, and even then it might be wrong the first five times we do it, but we keep adjusting every time, right?
And, but we're trying to softly create this, this commitment that I'm open to this new way of seeing myself, seeing myself in this case as somebody who's taking action and using the vehicle I'm about to show them before I ever show it to them.
So we've already opened the door to a new identity with commitment.
I find objections are the number one thing that causes a sale not to occur.
And so, you know, and objections for the most part are, you know, you, you said it best really, I can't say it any better.
These are the false beliefs that hold them back.
There's very rarely a case where an objection is true.
So this is an, this is interesting.
So, so Kevin Harrington, they just hired us to write their webinar pitch.
They did this launch.
I don't know if you saw it was a secrets of closing the sales, like a Zig Ziglar deal, right?
So, so he partnered with a guy named Mark Tim, a really cool guy who acquired the rights to be able to sell all of Zig's materials.
And so him and Kevin worked on this new product and they launched it.
They did a million dollar launch.
It just closed up a week or two ago.
And then I said, after that launch is done, come to me and we're going to take this, we're going to make it a webinar.
And first, the first thing we're going to do is sell it to all the people who said no, because, you know, if you close 10% of an audience and out of a hundred thousand people, that's 90,000 people who haven't said yes yet.
And I know that if I can give them new information with a new identity, I can double sales in a lot of cases to the same audience who just said no to something.
Cause I don't believe people say no.
I believe people don't have enough information yet to say yes.
And I don't know if that's kind of just the same thing, right?
But it helps me a lot.
I say, there's no no's.
There's yes today or maybe yes tomorrow.
And let me figure out how to make maybe yes tomorrow, actual yes tomorrow.
And then of course we're now going to roll this out to other people.
So here's what they did.
They sent me because Kevin sent out this email after the cart closed and he says, hey, you didn't invest.
I'd like to know why.
Essentially, that's what he said.
Now, by the way, if you believe the answers people give to that, you're lost.
You will lose.
You know, if you actually believe the answers that people are giving as to why they didn't buy and then you try to solve them directly, overtly, it's you're not going to sell them, period.
Because you're not going to give them any new information.
They've already thought about it and they don't know why they didn't really buy.
It's important that we understand that.
So here's one of the most common objections that I saw and I was laughing as I was reading it and I was starting to form my identities from this is it was like, I just, you know, I've already bought this other program and I have so much invested in effort and time to this that I can't do this right now with you.
Kevin, right?
Essentially, that's what it was.
Um, actually here, let me do this.
Let me, I'm not going to, I can't show it on the screen.
Well, let me move that out of there.
Um, but I've actually started to make notes.
So I'll see if I can, I can read one that really looks.
So here's one.
I tend to sign up for things, but then never allocate time to do them.
Working on setting my time up more productively.
So here's how most of you and how I used to solve this objection.
I would say here, here's what you're going to do.
When you sign up for this thing today, here's how I'm going to help you succeed.
So that way you don't fall a victim of buying a program and not allocating time for it.
Here's how I'm going to help you make sure you allocate time.
I've lost.
You think, oh, that's good.
Jason, is it?
No, it sucks.
That's how, that's how you lose a sale right there.
I don't know if it's obvious.
It's probably not obvious.
Why?
You know, the idea though is this person is essentially saying, if you're decoding what they're saying is, you know, when they say, I tend to sign up for things, but never allocate time to do them.
That's an identity issue.
They identify as somebody who doesn't follow through with things.
They identify as somebody who tends to impulsively make a decision and pay money for something and never getting around to doing it.
And that's true for everything you sell, by the way.
But if I were to see this objection come up five or 10 or 15 more times in the 60 pages of emails that he sent me, and I'm reading every single one of them, right?
I would say, okay, here's, here's how we shift this identity.
Now this is an objection.
So in the introduction, I'm already going to make grounds to shifting that identity.
Right?
I would, this is the frame I would try to pursue and pull off.
I would like them to believe that regardless of whether they make it a priority or not, how can this still benefit them for the money that they invest in it?
Now I don't know if I could pull that position off, but I want to create a situation where somebody's looking at it and say, Hey, even after the first week, if I quit logging into this program, again, like I do with every other stupid program I buy, I'm still going to be happy with the results.
If I can pull that off, I don't have to shift behavior.
I just shift perspective related to that behavior.
And if, and now I'm not going to do that just in the introduction, I'm going to do it throughout the whole training as well.
But this is how we kind of bring up objections very quietly and very quickly.
So one of the ways I might bring it up in the, in the introduction, I don't know what I'm going to do.
I still haven't figured out how I'm going to pitch this webinar yet, but I might say, and that's, it's, it's a, and if you find yourself in a situation where sometimes you invest in things, but you still don't use them and see change for them, I'm going to show you why that's not even an issue today.
Wouldn't it be wonderful if you didn't have to worry about, do I use this or do I not use this?
What would be wonderful?
It automatically happened like breathing, like blinking of the eyes, like the beating of the heart.
I'm not sure if we can do that today, but that's one of my goals.
How does that land with you?
Right?
Oh, I want to hear that.
You know, here's the biggest one though that I saw.
Um, let me see.
The biggest one that I saw.
Oh yeah.
Here are we one specifically.
What held me back was that I recently invested in several online learning and development limited time offers.
So here's what the average analysis on this.
Well, this is a person who's just spent a lot of money, who's already got a lot on their plate and I can't sell them because they just, you know, they just told me I didn't buy this because it's not mine.
Here's what immediately went off in my mind, Russell.
We got a buyer on our hands because a buyer is a buyer is a buyer.
People who buy a lot tend to buy even more.
These are the easiest people in the world to sell.
Uh, I got really excited when I saw these types of objections because I just have to show them why whatever they bought was way inferior to the thing I'm going to sell them.
And I firmly believe that what we have to offer to this audience is absolutely the case.
So what this tells me is they didn't do a good enough job of making that true.
They didn't do a good enough job in their sales process of showing them that regardless of whatever they bought in the past, it doesn't matter because this is going to be better than whatever they bought in the past.
That was not accomplished.
And so we have to, I would, I would handle that in the opener even.
I would say, listen, and you might be sitting here today thinking to yourself, Jason, this is all fine and good.
I've already made lots of commitments to bettering myself in the ways of selling and marketing and growing my online business.
And so while I'm interested in, I'm also skeptical that I'm able to make room for new information, no matter how good it is.
Now, by the way, that's a covert way of handling an objection.
I just said to them, do you agree with, hey, this, this could cure cancer, but I'm too busy, so I'm just going to die instead.
Right?
They're going to say, no, no, no, Jason, I, I will make time if it's good enough.
I didn't say that to them, but I allowed them to say it to me, right?
But more, more important, even than that is when you say something like that, then it opens up the idea of, is it a smart decision for me to limit myself based on my past purchasing behavior, if I'm presented with a really good opportunity.
So we're, we're chipping away at this impoverished identity up front, and then we're going to crush it through the content.
And now here's the other thing too, and I won't spend too much more time on this because we have a lot more to cover, but it's hard for somebody who sees a tomorrow is not any better than it today to want to invest in anything.
Period.
People, and people buy things to get out of pain.
That's the primary reason why they purchase things, to get out of pain.
But you don't want to just have them run away from something.
A serious salesperson has them run away from pain, but run into pleasure.
And now you're, you're using leverage from both ends, the pain and the pleasure at the same time.
And so this creates a new identity though.
This isn't an accident, right?
If they can see themselves tomorrow as being happy and excited, they can see themselves outside of the pain they're currently experiencing.
As somebody who doesn't experience that anymore, we are now subtly creating an identity shift, and then the entry gets them to stay and pay attention.
Why we do all of our voodoo magic into the mind, into the persuasion.
You know, and now my thing is very similar to yours, Russell, here is, is what's the one thing they will now be able to do that they couldn't do before?
That's an identity shift, but that's the one big outcome.
You call it the big domino, right?
I call it the clearly defined outcome.
I just tell them, I say, by the end of today, here's what you're going to be able to do that you couldn't do before essentially.
Cause now that gives us a target to both aim at it, gives me the target to aim up for my content that I'm going to create, but it also gives them the target of focusing on, will I be able to do this?
Can I see myself doing that?
That's an identity.
Now, the step by step, we kind of talked about that is here's what you need to do to achieve this outcome, but it's all done again with identity.
That's why before we ever teach them anything, we set the context for it.
You know, my favorite example of this, Russell, is people will say all the time, I want to lose weight.
You say, okay, give me your arm.
I'll chop it off.
You just lose 10 pounds right there.
And they say, well, hold on a second.
You say, well, you just told me you wanted to lose weight.
What's the big deal here?
They said, well, no, no, no, no, no, no.
You said, so there's more to the story than you don't just want to lose weight.
Right.
Tell me more, you know, so context is key.
Because again, if you show them something, but they don't understand the importance of it, then it's not going to land with nearly the amount of impact.
Now, if the context is forged around an identity, we do this for each step.
So we say step number one is this.
Now, here's why it's important that you understand why we do the step in this fashion.
Right.
Now, vision is the second part that we do for each step.
And this is really interesting because this is all identity shifting.
And it's just funny because even in my genius webinars program, Russell, I don't talk about this like we're talking about this right now.
This is exciting to me to get into the deeper things is the vision is if they can see themselves in their mind taking action on something, then it's far more likely for them to actually see themselves doing it in the real world.
Now, the ClickFunnels webinar does a good job of this, by the way, because you show them what the funnel looks like.
You show them the process and they can see themselves having a similar funnel.
Okay.
Now, this is really distinct and I don't think a lot of people get this.
You don't teach them how to build a funnel.
You don't say first push this button and then push this thing.
I mean, you kind of do, right?
But that's not the point.
The point isn't for them to learn how to build the funnel.
The point is for them to see themselves as somebody before who couldn't even get one, one, one, one webpage done.
Now they're getting all the web pages done.
And what more important than that, that's just a feature.
What's the outcome?
Now, why everybody else is sweating bullets staying up late at night, still pushing a button and messing with CSS and WordPress and trying to get this thing to look like that.
It's just looking ugly and then it's cooked, elite, and they give up, right?
You're on pro level.
You're doing the same stuff that Tony Robbins is doing out there on the web.
You're doing the same stuff of whoever their affinity is towards in that audience.
Your stuff looks even better than their stuff and you're doing it one-tenth of the time while they're spending thousands and thousands of dollars a day on an IT team.
Now they see a vision of themselves as being clever and crafty and smart and intelligent and better than the people that they worship.
That's powerful.
And so that's the vision.
And then of course the strategy is actually what to do.
That's the simplest thing.

All right.
So, so here's the philosophy.
If it's a, if the higher the ticket, the more likely you are to want to put the price up from.
And this comes back to the psychology of the easiest way to handle an objection is to agree with it, not to argue against it.
So somebody says the price is too high.
So I'm going to go back to the slide.
I'm going to argue against it.
So somebody says the price is too high.
You say absolutely to the wrong person, the price is totally too high, but to the right person, it's the best deal you could ever get.
I didn't disagree that the price is high.
I just reframed how I positioned that, right?
And so the beautiful thing is because you're going to get sticker shock on high price points, you can wait to get the sticker shock at the end.
Or if you put it in the beginning, they're going to say, I'm not going to buy it for that price.
That's fantastic.
Because it's not going to be that price at the end when you drop it.
But we've already gotten the sticker shot out of the way because all price is relative, by the way.
Nobody can envision 10,000, the number 10,000 in their mind.
Can't do it.
Can't.
The only way you can do it is make it relative to something.
So if I said, you know, there was a forest fire in California where I'm at and it burned 8,000 acres, you're like, what's that mean?
Right?
But if I said, imagine putting 10 football fields side by side, that's how much it burned.
I got it.
Right?
And so nobody knows how much $10,000.
Is this $10,000 a lot?
Well, it depends.
If I gave you a freaking brand new Ferrari for $10,000, that's not a lot of money.
Is it?
That's a little amount of money.
But if I sold you a Tootsie roll for $10,000, it better have diamonds and gold at it because otherwise that's way too much.
So all price is relative, right?
Relative to what?
Relative to itself even.
$10,000 sounds good if you thought it was $25,000.
You're not thinking about it's $10,000.
You're thinking about I'm saving $15,000.
So the high price point becomes an actual massive benefit in that regard.
And you want to put that up front.
Now, typically most of the things we sell on webinars usually range between, you know, 200 to $2,000.
And so in that case, we typically don't reveal the price up front.
We reveal the price and then we immediately drop it.
But if you're selling a high ticket, what are you selling out?
At Grant's thing.
Three grand.
Three grand.
Three grand, right?
So here's what I would do instead and I very often do this.
So if you look at my slides here, one of the objections is always money.
That's a universal objection, right?
And so if I feel like for a majority, and this is always a judgment call for anybody.

Don't they pay a lot of money?

So everybody in that room's at least paid a thousand or two.
What to speak of travel expenses, hotel stays, everything, right?
And so to me, I wouldn't even be too concerned with price because these people have demonstrated through behavior that they're not super price sensitive.
They're going to be more interested in, can I get the best deal?
So if I was selling this audience, I would engineer, and by the way, this is important and everybody listening, make careful note of this is the one of the most powerful things about a good sales pitch webinar or on stage doesn't really matter.
And Russell's already demonstrated that he seeks the same pitch on stage, puts it in a webinar and just prints more money that way.
It's fantastic.
Price on stage, that's the only thing I change.
That's the, yeah, I mean, stage, stage is a more effective way of selling than a webinar.
Let's be very clear for everybody here, but the trade off is the opportunity costs and the time commitment.
And the fact is most stages aren't worth speaking on because they don't bring enough people for people like us, right?
But you know, in this particular case, you say, first of all, you look at the situation, there's going to be 8,000 people here.
Most of them paid a grand, if not more, if ever there was a time to stack and make the offer even more ridiculous.
Now here's the balance.
Without invalidating or pissing off existing customers who've already done business with you, what would that offer be?
Or what could that offer be?
Is there anything special just for this audience that we could do that otherwise we couldn't?
So I would put more emphasis on the value add less on the price view because the value add is going to go crazy for them.
So here's a close we do on stage and it might, it won't make sense probably for this audience this time, but it might make sense.
Is this, here's the biggest problem with selling from stage in my opinion is people say, okay, I buy this thing and then I go home and then I might see the benefit from it sometime in the future.
Whereas if they buy an online course, they get immediate access to it.
So one of the things we always ask is how do we make immediate access on a live event?
But I got this for the webinar technique because sometimes we do these things as if you buy before the end of the webinar, then you get this special bonus or the price will go up if you wait until after the webinar.
If we really want to push sales, we front end the sales for the webinar itself.
Most of the time we don't do that because we make more money on the replays and we'll talk more about funnels here in a little bit, but there are certain instances where that might make sense.
So in this particular case, like in a seminar, this is how we would sell it.
We were selling this one thing.
This was the pitch.
That's where I came up with this.
It's like a trip to us with China.
So we have this guy who goes over to China.
He goes to this marketplace over there.
He sources products and he sells them like crazy.
And essentially we're doing guided tours with translators, the whole 9s, and it was five grand a person from the stage, right?
I was selling five grand, but I'm like, man, they're going to buy this thing for China and then they're going to be two months later.
They're going to get on an airplane and go to China.
And then they get the product at China and then they come home.
It's going to be another two months before they see the result.
I'm selling something that if all goes well, maybe three months from now, they'll see a massive benefit.
It's like, oh, this sucks.
Right.
So here's what we did.
We essentially says, listen, and the training starts tonight in my suite.

Uh, yes.
You can get wild in Vegas, even if you're a morbid, you know, so it's like there's a lot of opportunity there in the sense of getting really nice presidential rooms or whatever.
I mean, the room we had at a two pianos and it not one, right?

And here's why this is important.
And again, with that big of an audience, you, it probably doesn't make sense to do this close in this direct application of it.
Uh, but, but this is an example of a value at, right?
Cause the idea is first of all, nobody wants to be the first person to do the back of the room.
Everybody looks around and they're like, I'll go buy if they go buy, right?
So the trick is how do we create that first initial stampede?
And this does that, uh, you know, cause then these people want to, want to go and they want to run to the back of the, what to buy.
The downside to this is if they're number 61, they're like, well, I don't want to buy anymore cause now I'm going to miss out.
Now you've created pain, right?
Or whatever the limitation of the room sizes.
So you either got to have a big enough room where you say, if we fill this up, I'm happy if nobody else buys, but you also have a, I always have a plan B in that particular case.
Plan B is always, is usually this depends on the promoter.
It depends on who you're working with, right?
But we run our own events mostly.

Yeah, we can't do that.

So, so those are just examples.
I would say in this case with grants audiences, what is something ridiculous, special for the occasion that can only happen by being in proximity with you there, that you're comfortable delivering.
That's the killer.
That thing, that, that will give more leverage in sales than anything will.
And then, you know, to apply it from an online perspective is like when you have a big enough affiliate or a big enough person for everybody listening, that's when you want to niche down.
That's when you want to focus and that's when you want to take the extra time to customize the presentation for that audience.
Because if Russell gets this pitch right next week, there are very few times this year, I'm going to assume we're going to present it with this amount of people to an audience that isn't yours with this buying power to have an impact and to make a lot of money.
So in that case, it's worth the extra effort.
And that's what's really great about a webinar.
But yeah, my point in that particular case for a $3,000 deal, I wouldn't say price upfront, but I would say, and here's what's really important.
And at the end, we do have this opportunity for those of you who are right fit to move forward with, we're going to really empower you.
And it's really important that you're not in the bathroom at that time, or that you're, you're not on the hallway, jaw boning and networking with somebody else.
Because nothing makes me more upset than somebody that says, Russell, oh, I missed out on this amazing thing that you were doing.
And I heard about it third hand from somebody else who got there and said, it was the best thing in their life.
So at the end, it's very important that you pay very close attention to what I'm going to do, because what I'm going to do is something that, and this is where we calibrate to the audience, right?
That if you're a salesperson and who hears a salesperson, well, by the way, everybody is, but who here says they're a salesperson.
If you're a salesperson, you can also steal what I'm going to do and use it yourself, and you could probably double sales just like that.

Oh man, he's, he's actually cool.
I really, uh, I went and I had a lunch with him when he came out last time, uh, in LA was interesting.
A Kyle says, do you, and I'm just going to run through these as quickly as I can and in reverse order.
Do you have specific strategies for testing price?
Kyle asks, I don't in the sense that the first thing we want to do is when we sell any new offer and any new format is we kind of go by just a guess.
We say this, you know, typically I like to sell products for a thousand dollars or $2,000, uh, certain products.
If they're the more mass market they are, the more likely we are to sell them for between $200 and $500.
So like weight loss offers are great at a $200 to $500 price point because you have millions of millions of people that could buy those.
But like, you know, hardcore business advice is, is for a very narrow audience.
So we want to start with a higher price point.
But typically what we're looking for there is we're looking to see, given the best strategy with what we feel is the maximum price point we can get for the highest amount of conversion.
Let's try it and see what happens.
And then based on the results, we can adjust it.
Now here's the caveat.
Uh, this is important.
I always start lower than I want the price to be at because here's the thing, you can always raise price, but it's very hard to lower the price because God forbid you sell really well and then you want to lower the price later.
You've just invalidated all your best customers who paid more money for you.
So you have to reinvent how the offer structure.
So the price is positioned differently or you raise your prices.
And so like, so the best thing to do in that situation is say, this should be selling at this price, I'm going to lower it a little bit, tell my audience, I'm doing that, tell them to plan on seeing the price increase sometime in the future.
And then if you do really well, raise the price.
If you didn't do really well, then, then you should probably just kill it anyway and try a different offer or a different approach to it or a different pitch for it.
Uh, yeah.
And by the way, you know, I don't think you'd eat ADD.
Honestly, I don't think ADD actually gives me any benefit that, uh, that also doesn't come with a disadvantage to it.
Joseph, what's the highest price you would sell out of webinar and when would you move?
Uh, so it depends on the audience.
So if you already have an existing audience who's bought a front end product for you, say a thousand or $2,000, I'll sell to that, that audience on a webinar, something that's five or 10 or even $15,000.
Uh, if they're bigger price points than that, typically we don't sell them on webinars and I will tell you why.
Uh, we don't sell them on webinars simply because we want to qualify the person.
If they, if the price point gets too high, we don't want to take any in everybody.
So that's when we will use the webinar.
I'm literally doing a training later today to our genius webinar customers on, uh, the consultant model of how to use webinars to set appointments.
And that's when we fill the price before they ever get on the call and use it in that fashion.
So we still use a webinar, but then it's a two step process, not a one step.
Everybody here will get a replay and we're going to give this to you as fast as possible.
And I believe there's a members area that we'll give you access to with this as well.
My team and Russell's team set that up.
So I'm not sure, uh, how are you talking, what do you talk about getting to the state?
Yeah.
Uh, this is, this is really important.
So state to me is everything.
Uh, so in my, my particular case, before I ever get on a webinar, I put myself in a state of gratitude first and foremost.
I say, this is what I'm grateful for.
You know, I'm always grateful for the opportunity, uh, to put myself out there and to try to help more people.
I also talk about, you know, we'll just want them a grateful for in general.
And then I say, what am I excited about?
What's really getting me going and getting me out of bed and moving in the morning.
And then I take those things and I really amplify them in my body.
And I really get, I feel really good about what I'm going to do.
And then I can hop on and then I can go.
And that automatically fixes tonality, all that kind of stuff.
Okay.
Joss, how do you get started practicing this before you have an offer or an expert?
Uh, that's a great question, John.
Here's what I recommend.
You don't wait till you're ready to get started, get started before you're ready.
And you say, well, if I don't have an offer or if I'm not an expert, uh, one of the, the most money I've ever made on webinars is selling other people's products, period.
So here's what you do to somebody.
You go to somebody who you like, respect and who you trust and who you think is powerful and has a solution to their audience that is underutilized currently.
And you say to them, this is the rookie approach.
And I'll give you the advanced approach.
The rookie approach is saying, if I write you a pitch to your audience, selling your product and making you look like the all star and the rock star that you are, would you be okay with for every $100?
This makes you, you otherwise wouldn't make, give me $20 of that.
That's option number one.
My option that I prefer instead is option number two, which is you write a pitch in advance and then show up to that person.
I wrote this for you.
Would you be open to running it to your list?
And then if you are, can we talk about revenue split?
Cause I'd like to make you a lot of money and then maybe have you give me a little money out of that.
This allows you to sell without having to wait for everything to get in order.
You know, people always say, Jason, how to get traffic to my webinar?

So what's the ideal conversion rate for webinar and personal moment?
So, um, it depends.
Conversion rates, not the right number.
I don't think cause there's a whole bunch of different variation variables, right?
They go into that of like, what do you spend me?
How much are you charging?
Like, um, and so conversion rates, I don't think there's a whole bunch of different variables, right?
And so conversion rates, the exact right number to look at that.
The number to look at is like ROI, right?
Like what's return on investment?
Cause I'll spend your $200 lead.
If it's the right lead coming in, who's going to buy the thing?
It's my conversion rate.
It's going to be horrible, but it's the right people, you know, and vice versa.
So what I'm like, I'm usually looking at, um, uh, when I'm doing any kind of webinar, if I'm running, if I'm running ads, right?
I'm looking at how much it costs me to get submitted to register.
Um, I like to be somewhere between the two and eight dollars to get somebody to register.
And I try to see how many people actually show up.
So my goal is typically to get at least 20% show up rate.
And then, um, the next time we look at it, what percentage do I close when I transition to, from my, from my, um, uh, from my thing to my, to my clothes, right?
So I'm not transitioning from, uh, my, my, my teaching to my clothes.
Since I started the transition, I was looking at how many people were live right then.
And then my goal, um, I would say if you close about 5% of those people, um, you've got a webinar, it's like a six figure webinar to fill us 10%.
You're a million dollar, your webinar, 15%, you're $10 million a year or above.
Um, and that's kind of the numbers I'm looking at.
So that's, that's kind of the conversion rate numbers.
And then my goals and from the replay sequence afterwards is to try to double as many cells as I made during the live webinar.
And those are my, my metrics that you run.
That's like our baseline metrics.
And so when we do a webinar, we buy ads, we drive traffic, we look at everything.
When webinars done, look at numbers.
Okay.
Was our ad cost between, you know, two and eight dollars?
Yes or no.
If no, like you had to change our ads and the messaging on the page, whatever that might be.
Well, to show up at least 20% yes or no.
If not, what do we need to do?
We need to add text messages and we need to do things like that.
Um, uh, do we, uh, um, are people staying, you know, how people are on the end?
Do I close 10% of those?
Do I close 15% or close five?
Like what am I doing there?
And then usually it's like, if I, if I close too low, it's usually one of three things.
Number one, you taught too much during the webinar and you'd have story telling, this kind of stuff.
Number two, um, uh, was the offer any good?
Number three was the urgency and scarcity.
Like those are kind of the three core things.
And we tweak around those until we get point where about 10% and 10% 10% closed rate on a webinar, 10% of the people who were on when you transition to close is awesome.
Um, that's where most of the webinars hit towards the funnel hackers.
Click phones one to 15% on a webinar and that became, you know, huge.
So if you 15% like it's game over, time to go have some fun.
Um, all right.
Next question.
Uh, questions are flying in now.
All right.
Uh, next question someone asked, um, how do you do a webinar, a live webinar twice, twice a week doing the audience get tired of it.
So my whole model is you do a webinar at least once a week or twice, whatever that is, but this is to new blood.
Okay.
The problem with most businesses is they have a list of customers, they created a webinar, they approach the customers and then now what do I do next week?
I have the same customers and they go and they approach it.
They come to approach it once, right?
That's not how you grow a business.
That's how you make some side money, but the way you grow a business is new blood, new people coming in, right?
Um, uh, one of my favorite stories, um, uh, Mary Ellen Tribee, she, uh, she's an amazing person.
She worked for a company called Weiss Publishing and she took it from, I think they're like $5 or $6 million a year.
She blips like 80 million.
And she went to you, Agora, took them from like 8 million to like 90 million in a year and a half.
And, uh, and I asked one day, I'm like, how do you grow companies from like 8 million to 80?
I gotta be cool to learn that process.
And she told me something that was really powerful.
She said, you internet marketing guys, you're so smart.
You, you do the equivalent of like, uh, a Broadway play where you go and you hire the best script writers and the best actors and the best screenwriters.
You practice your rehearsed, you make this perfect play slash funnel, right?
It's perfect thing.
And then you go and, uh, you do opening night, you advertise all over everywhere, but because you live in Boise, Idaho, you open on Boise and you launch it and people come and they sign for the play.
They come, uh, you know, they love the whole thing is smashing succession, make a bunch of money and then it closes down.
And then the first thing you guys do is like, okay, let's write another play.
And she's all I do is I take you to the internet marketing guys.
I tell you, don't write another play.
Instead, let's go take this play on the road.
She takes your play that she's like, I take their play that's working and I take it from Boise, Idaho to Chicago and then LA and then New York.
And I take it on the road.
And that's the difference between like what, what you guys are doing, making four or five million bucks a year, right?
You need to make, you know, $100 million a year.
And that was like my, aw, my life hold moment.
And so I came back and said, all right, I'm going to do this webinar live every single week.
Uh, but that means that we had to get good at buying ads and get good at getting people to show up because I can't show us the same audience every single time.
I need new blood every single week.
And so that became the focal point is like, how do we fill the webinar every single week?
Um, and how do you just make it through week and five a week?
And so I do my exact same webinar word for word, other than my tweaks I make as I learn each time, word for word, every single week or twice a week or three times a week or five times a day, whatever it is.
And my goal is get new blood into this thing.
Okay.
And when you look at that, you're shifting your perspective of business from like, I have this, my list, I've wrote my webinar list and I got to write a new webinar because my list is seen now.
No, wait, make one webinar and this is how you start growing your list.
They start growing your company.
That's how we went, you know, from when we launched ClickFundals, I had maybe 150, 200,000 people on the list.
Now we have, you know, millions on list with that 57,000 people or customers.
I couldn't have done that.
Just messaging the same webinar to my list.
If I'm going out looking for new blood all the time, growing it, growing it, growing it, and it shifts your mentality.
And then this is our model here is is every week or every twice a week, however often you're doing it, you're, you're bringing people in.
And then once a quarter, you do the webinar to the existing list you've been bringing in, but by then they've already forgot about it.
And we do a webinar every time to the webinar, make a million dollar webinar every single time to the existing audience.
So once the quarter, I did my webinar to the entire list.
And then other than that, I'm just bringing new blood into the webinar, doing the webinar, doing the one quarter, boom, big payday webinar, webinar, webinar, one supporter, boom, payday.
And that's, that's the, um, that's the process.
And so that's awesome.
You're going to have to shift your thinking from like, my list, I saw the webinar, I need to make a new webinar to like, no, be, be, be disciplined, like make a webinar and then take it on the road.
And that's how you scale a company.
Cause I mean, Jason and I both written insane amounts of webinars, but I tell you what, the, the 500 webinars I wrote in the first 10 years of my business made me a lot less than the one I wrote that I took on the road.
Yeah.
I have one webinar that's made me over $45 million in sales, right?
It's like, and then I got every other webinar combined.
That's made me $55 million in sales, essentially, right?
Wagers do one over and over again, huh?
Take the show on the road.

I don't think a lot of people realize this.
When I started Rapid Crush, my company in 2011, we started exclusively for the first year and a half selling software.
Everybody thinks you can't sell software on webinars and everybody thinks that it's only info products or coaching programs is anything, by the way.
But I started selling software because I got tired of just teaching people things and then not having a vehicle to use it.
So I'm like, I'll do the education and then I'll sell them the software.
And it's a magic push button solution.
How stupid I was because then they get the software like, how do I use it?
You know, like a back of the info product business.
But I learned so much on how to sell software from webinars.
It's a lot different in a lot of ways in selling a traditional thing.
And so like what you did, Russell, with, with ClickFunnels, it's so brilliant is, and I don't think people get this.
And this is about offers and creative ideas.
You don't sell ClickFunnels on that webinar, do you?
I did the first two or three webinars and then one time I did the offer and it was like, boom.
And, okay, so fun.
Okay.
So this is, this is the big thing.
And I felt probably 20 people who sell software do this now and it crushes on everyone.
So if you watch the webinar and hopefully you've seen my webinar at least once, if not go watch it like a thousand times because you'll learn from it.
But, by the end, by the time this is done, they believe webinar or they believe that funnels are the greatest thing in the world.
And then, and then they want this ClickFunnels thing I demoed because I think it's awesome, but they like, I pay monthly fees.
So I'm like, so when I try and just then close, I'm like, how many guys would like to get ClickFunnels for free for the next six months, the next 12 months?
And they all start going crazy.
Like, okay, cool.
I'm sure how to get ClickFunnels for free.
When you invest in my training program called Funnel Hacks or whatever you call it, Funnel Hacks, then I'm going to give you 12 month access to ClickFunnels for free.
I mean, he's no monthly payments.
And so the entire thing, like, if you look at my mindset and everything I sell, I want everything I sell to be free always.
Right.
So if I'm selling a book, it's like, Hey, free book.
Right.
And then the upsell is like, Hey, if you buy the audio book, I'll give you these 12 courses for free.
And that's like, Hey, if you buy, I try to use like everything's free all the time.
Right.
And so for me, like the thing I'm selling on the webinar is the training program, the eight or 10 or six week program.
Right.
And if you get that, you get all the stuff for free, you get ClickFunnels for free.
You get, now we have the new pitch.
You get Funnel Scripts for free.
You get Traffic Seekers for free.
Like everything else becomes free.
All you do is invest in this one training course for three grand is tiny.
You get all this other stuff for free, free, free, free.
And so when we transition the offer from like, buy the software for six months, people like trying to, uh, it's like, Hey, just buy this other thing over here.
You get software for free.
Now it's a sexy offer.
Right.
Um, like, uh, one of my upsells, we tested a whole bunch of things.
It was an upsell that had like, I can't remember which book, but it had like, uh, five or six days worth of event videos.
It was awesome.
So I, as the creator of this thing, I'm like, Oh, you're going to get this and we have five days of training.
You get all these kinds of, and like the conversions sucked on it.
Right.
Cause people are like, Oh, I have days of training.
That's a lot of work.
Yeah.
Obvious.
So what I did, and we're going to talk about this here, how I do my clothes, how do my clothes is the thing I call, I call the stack.
I learned from arm and more.
And in this five day event, I spent like 30 minutes teaching the stack.
And so I had this idea when the upsell was converting, I'm like, what if I just talk about like one thing?
And so I was like, Hey, this is the deal guys.
There's this thing I taught.
I did a big event and I taught this one thing.
It was 30 minute session called the stack.
And this is the results of the stack.
I talked about how amazing the stack is.
And they're like, I need another stack of my kids to do.
If you, if you buy this training on the stack, it's going to be 97 bucks.
I'm going to give you the other four and a half days worth of training for free.
And all of a sudden they're like, Oh, and the conversion went from like two and a half percent conversion rate to like 14 and a half percent conversion rate, which in my world is tens of millions of dollars.
And so by, by, by shifting the offer.
And so yeah, we shifted the offer from buy click funnels to buy this training program and get click funnels for free.
And I can say free like 8,500 times in my stack, which is very important for me.
Well, but, but the free thing you spend 95% of your time focusing on that's the secret.
Absolutely.
The stuff they pay for most of you, this is the joke I said to my audience after they bought click funnels.
I said, most of you don't even realize the thing that you actually bought was funnel hacks.
You probably even look at that trading.
No, of course not.
Right.
It was a convenient excuse for them to get click funnels.
They didn't buy the software.
They bought a solution.
Click funnels was positioned as free and this goes against every conventional wisdom that's out there.
It's like, wait a minute, you spend 99% of your time hyping up and selling the thing they get for free.
And you spend one year, one percent of your time on the actual thing that they're supposedly paying for.
How did you figure out how to do that?
I want to know kind of what led you to that insight and then that execution of positioning it like that.
Yeah, it was three years ago, we made the pitch.
I just remember we'd done the webinar a couple of times and every time I pitched it where I was pitching the software, there were so many questions that started coming in the question box.
Like, well, how long does it work?
And how would like, like there are people had so many questions are like these like annoyances when they were buying the software because they're like the price shopping and convenience shopping in this and feature.
And I also started coming when they thought that's what they're buying.
And I got tired of like, I get that part in the stack and I try to resolve the concerns and it was just so much.
And just one of the one of the times I was like, I'm going to just I'm going to try this the other way.
And when I did that, all those things disappeared.
Like suddenly they didn't they weren't price shopping because they were getting it for free.
And that it just it cleared up and it simplified the pitch so much free for a limited time.
Right.
Yeah, free for the next, you know, the first time we did it the first year we was free for a year for a year.
Yeah, I know.
I wish I would have promoted it back then.
We should have free for six months and the conversion I didn't drop.
And we keep talking about it was free for three months.
But I kind of like six month things.
I guess people have time to use the software and stuff.
But yeah, like free for six months.
Because the other thing that was the problem is like, then people are asking like, well, then how much is it going to cost each month and month and you know, all stuff and I was able to be like, look, I love how you close that, by the way, you're like, oh, we'll figure that out when it comes.
It's not a big deal.
Why don't you make some money first and then we'll get to that.
Right.
You're bypassing all the issues.
That's one of my big secrets is like throughout the pitch, I'm always trying to delay the buying decision for them.
Right.
And so like this is like, it's free.

We have found a way to position free with high ticket offers, continuity programs as affiliates for physical products, for digital products.
But the whole mechanism is the biggest appeal, the thing that gets people the most excited is the fact that they're getting something for free.
They're paying, but they're getting something for free.
And so I think the biggest powerful thing that we can all do is figure out what we can in our webinar sell for free as a bonus or as kind of a misdirection.

I'd like to hear your theory on why that's so effective is it's just the focus from, here's what I pay to, here's the value I get.
And I think that shift in focus makes people feel like I'm getting all this stuff and it's so powerful.
It's so valuable.
Not I have to pay.
I have to, I have to give this.
I have to sacrifice this.
And that one shift, if anybody can do that for anything that they sell is flip that switch around.
I think that is just as powerful as any of these like minutiae techniques that we come up with, and which is why we're talking about the offer here right now.

Continuity is way harder.
And here's why, by the way.
And this goes to offer complexity.
The harder it is for you to explain your offer to somebody, the harder it is for them to pay you money for it.
You know, and you have to be very careful with offer complexity.
Because if you do it right, it can be really powerful because you can re-engineer how people look at your offer and you can have some value levers that you can pull.
Right?
But if it takes you 30 minutes just to unpack how they can do business with you, that's 30 minutes you can't spend on objection smashing and commitment elicitation and value stacking and all this kind of stuff.
Right?
And so the easiest thing is in a situation like this, like in the SAS thing, is you cite stuff all that, they don't get into the minutiae and the details, but all continuities like that.
Because here's all the objections that come up.
Well, how do I cancel?
And the other objection is, well, gee, I'm going to end up paying for this forever unless otherwise noted.
And I mean, and if you think about all the objections related to continuity, it's fast.
It's massive, you know?
And so this is a decide.

Or you could pay $500 a month to use this funnel in perpetuity.
Now, this is called red herring pricing.
We tried to create a situation where price juxtaposition.
Do I want to pay a whole bunch of money?
Or do I not want to pay a whole bunch of money?
It makes that value look good.

Because instead of just taking care of this all at once for $2,500 or $2,499, right?

So you're going to pay one way or another.

My whole friend, I did it way better than I just described it there, but my whole frame was you're an idiot if you pay thousands and thousands and thousands of dollars extra for the exact same thing you could get for $2,500.
